name: Average Rates of Profit in US Manufacturing, 1960–1989

book_quotes.quote: Figure 2.12 depicts the average profit rates of sectors within US manufacturing, with the heavy line representing that of the manufacturing sector as a whole (chapter 7 and appendix 7.1). We can see that turbulence is normal to profitability.

Figure 2.12 Average Rates of Profit in US Manufacturing, 1960–1989. Figure 2.13 Incremental Rates of Profit in US Manufacturing, 1960–1989. Discussed in chapter 7 in relation to figure 7.14. Appendix 7.2 Data Tables for Chapter 7 (available online at http://www.anwarshaikhecon.org/)

primary_source.agency: Constructed (Shaikh / Christodoulopoulos) from OECD / BEA industry-level data
primary_source.table_or_series_id: Sectoral USMANAVG + 14 manufacturing sub-sectors (USACHE, USAMAI, USAFOD, USAMNM, USAMIO, USATEX, USABMI, USAMEL, USAWOD, USAMEQ, USAMTR, USAPAP, USABMA, USAMOT)
primary_source.available_through: Appendix 7.2 data tables (book companion site); OECD STAN

formula: r_sector = profit / capital_stock per sector (full definition in Appendix 7.1)

components.label: Sectoral profit (numerator) for 15 US manufacturing aggregates

extension_candidates.subseries_label: OECD STAN sectoral data + BEA GDP-by-industry + Fixed-Asset Tables
concerns: Sectoral codes (USACHE, USAMAI, etc.) follow OECD ISIC — may need crosswalk to NAICS for modern extension. Series is illustrative for 1960–1989 and not central to RSCD's main empirical claims.

methodology_notes: Formula series: 15 manufacturing-sector profit rates (14 sub-sectors + USMANAVG aggregate).
Full methodology in Appendix 7.1 and Christodoulopoulos (1995, 138–140); see also Shaikh (1998b, 395).
Replication-fidelity series: focus is the 1960–1989 window for which Shaikh's analysis was done.
open_questions: OECD STAN sector codes have evolved since 1989; building a faithful modern extension requires careful concordance to NAICS that may exceed Phase 3 scope.
